James R. G. Wright, 'Virgil's Pastoral Programme: Theocritus, Callimachus, and Eclogue 1,' PCPS 209 (1983) 107-58.
Filimon's article provides a German summary for those who do not read
Romanian. Havas makes yet another vain effort to detect political realities
within the Bucolics. Kenney's helpful article treats Eclogues 2 and 8 with
attention to their elegiac character and their relevance to the process of
literary creation.
Wright detects a programme of pastoral poetry which is
specifically Roman with indebtedness to Theocritus, Callimachus and Hesiod.

345 f.,and A. 12.519f. Pöschl's address, as always, sheds light on a neglected aspect of Vergilian style. Schmid's volume probes problems and cruces
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:11:30 UTC
68
with vigour and determination. Vergil
is credited with the authorship of
Culex, Catalepton, and Moretum, along with ille ego,andAíarzíua me genuit.
The Eclogues are studied for structure, cryptograms and the like. Acrostichs,
mesostichs and telestichs are part and parcel of Latin poetry, but the 'clues'
can lead to unlikely assertions: e.g., that the Aeneid was dedicated to Antonius Sosius! Schmid offers a wealth of information and suggestion for the
wary. Willcock's study of battle conventions, the presentation of a warrior's
aristeia, and the identification of minor warriors is highly serviceable and
enlightening. Deryck Williams' Sussex lecture interprets the main themes of
the epic and concludes that the poem is an exploration of contrasting viewpoints of human life. T. R. Glover's phrase, There is a wavering about the
poem* seems particularly apt in light of modern criticism of the epic. Wiltshire's article detects a tension between public and private realms in the
Aeneid that is maintained and eased by the capacity of some characters to act
in the public realm at a distance from the self. Courage, truthfulness (devotion and piety) help to preserve the public realm ;self-distancing is an essential
virtue. Her bimillenary address constructs an intellectual framework for a
reading of Vergil in our time.

Francesco Giancotti, 'Lettura del dodicesimo libro dell'Eneide,' LECTURAE VERGILIANAE 391-524.
Camps' lecture on Aeneid I is a perceptive study of the design and content.
Fredricksmeyer's article attacks the poem's primary historical meaning
in the propositio (1-7) and invocatio (8-11) where thematic and programmatic attitudes and values are sounded. The thematic issues are (1) the principle of teleology, and (2) the problem of theodicy. Both motifs in combination are, as Fredericksmeyer reminds us, unprecedented in classical literature. Gross explores the transformations of gaza
from A.
1.119 and 2.763-4,
to 5.40 (gaza . . . agresti). Harrison's bimillenary lecture explores the adventitious appearances of Mercury in the epic. Knox detects a recasting of aspects
of the Argonautica and the Odyssey in obstipuit primo aspectu Sidónia Dido.
Sarkissian assesses
the storm
of Aeneid 1 as a reflection of the political
atmosphere of the late Republic; Aeolus is seen as a trespassing magistrate,
guilty of violating impérium. The sequel, the sedition simile, and Jupiter's
prophecy of Rome's Augustan domination (257-96), strengthens the political
'reading.' Gransden's articles on Troy's fall explore language and style, adaptation from the cyclic Little Iliad, the non-Vergilian character of the Helen
episode, and analogies between Sinon and Iago. Hardie traces repeated applications of motifs from the Lucretian sacrifice of Iphigenia in the Sinon episode (A.
2.57-198), the Death of Laocoon (A. 2.199-231), and the omen
attaching to Jůlus (A. 2.679-91). Zintzen's monograph enriches the understanding of the Laocoon episode. Basto's study of weaponry in A. 4 provides
fresh insights into the Dido and Aeneas episode. Farron demonstrates that
Vergil's contemporaries were much more sentimental, romantic, and emotional than people today. She also treats the startling oxymoron "she has
been watching him without looking at him" (R. D. Williams, ad. loc.). Farron
rejects 'hostile' (A. 6.469 underscores her refusal to interact - 'with her back
towards him'), and explores the connotations of aversa as an extreme instance of Vergilian ambiguity and compression, initially incomprehensible.
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:11:30 UTC
71
Moles returns to A. 4.124-5 and the irrelevant problem of 'who seduced
whom in the cave.' Dux femina facti (Л. 1.364) is banished as parallel; Dido
dux et Troianus offers a post-positive usage; Aeneas is described as dux Troianus because Juno aspires to the union of Dido and Aeneas as guarantee of
the union of their respective peoples (cf. 4.99-104). Brenk's assessment of the
Palinurus incident is bold and persuasive. Monaco's article on spectator response in the funeral games of Anchises suggests future lines of inquiry.
D'Elia's lecture on the Sixth Book treats, inter alia, the topography of the
Phlegraean Fields and the penumbral character of the entire book. Gotoff's
impressionistic reading of Aeneid 6 assesses the passage through the gate of
false dreams as VergiPs way of erasing knowledge of Roman history from
Aeneas' mind. He cannot retain his knowledge of the future of Rome and so
continues to move in a haze of uncertainty and unreality after the descent.
Perret argues that, based on the testimony of Valerius Flaccus and Statius, A.
6.602-7 and 616-20 have exchanged their original places. Putnam explores the
iconography of Romulus and the implications of suo . . . honore (A. 6.790).
Augustus is the new Romulus, the victor Quirinus (cf. G. 3.27), 'exalted for
acts of war. Tatum studies the allusive links of Dido with Caeneus, Heracles
of Argonautica 4, and Ajax.
Fredricksmeyer treats the concentric arrangement of corresponding sections in the central part of Book 7 (the Allecto
scene). The arrangement extends to the two outer parts of the book: the first
and third sections contain correspondences to each other in their entirety and
in their individual sections. Harrison examines Aen. 8.572-4, and suggests that
Evander appeals as an Arcadian to Jupiter's own Arcadian origins. Michel's
study of A.
8
is valuable; it includes an examination of the shield (pp.
288-90). La Penna's Vergilian lecture treats the Nisus and Euryalus episode at
length (303-15). Hornsby treats the final scene of the epic. He notes that
although anger and action have caught the attention of critics, speech and
sight are inextricably bound to both. Aeneas is sensitive to the uses and
limitations of speech and sight but Turnus is unaware and uses them in a less
sophisticated,
ineffectual
manner.
Kihsey
argues
that
the foedus
(A.
12.242-3 : foedusque . . . infectum) was not concluded and that infectum cannot denote 'cancellation' or 'undone.' Losada assesses the brief but arresting
picture of the hero frustrated by the vivid, biting stump. The intervention of
Venus
underscores
the essentially
human
nature
of Aeneas.
Giancotti's
lengthy
review
of problems and scholarship
attaching
to Aeneid
12
is
thorough and provocative.

title: Camps' lecture on Aeneid I is a perceptive study of the design and content. Fredricksmeyer's article attacks the poem's primary historical meaning in the propositio (1-7) and invocatio (8-11) where thematic and programmatic attitudes and values are sounded. The thematic issues are (1) the principle of teleology, and (2) the problem of theodicy. Both motifs in combination are, as Fredericksmeyer reminds us, unprecedented in classical literature. Gross explores the transformations of gaza from A. 1.119 and 2.763-4, to 5.40 (gaza . . . agresti). Harrison's bimillenary lecture explores the adventitious appearances of Mercury in the epic. Knox detects a recasting of aspects of the Argonautica and the Odyssey in obstipuit primo aspectu Sidónia Dido. Sarkissian assesses the storm of Aeneid 1 as a reflection of the political atmosphere of the late Republic; Aeolus is seen as a trespassing magistrate, guilty of violating impérium. The sequel, the sedition simile, and Jupiter's prophecy of Rome's Augustan domination (257-96), strengthens the political 'reading.' Gransden's articles on Troy's fall explore language and style, adaptation from the cyclic Little Iliad, the non-Vergilian character of the Helen episode, and analogies between Sinon and Iago. Basson's treatment of Mezentius is a companion piece for Gotoff's more
venturesome study of the 'paradoxical character* of the Etruscan exile. Gotoff concentrates on Vergilian invention, and on the scenes, vignettes, and
nuances that affect the character of the antagonistic hero and the culminating
event. Harrison's Sussex lecture studies Juno's hostility to Rome and its
extension after the reconciliation of A. 12. Vergil keeps Juno ignorant of the
ultimate ruin of Carthage by the Romans; she does not have access to the
prophecies of Anchises, and her efforts to save her beloved Carthage are
thwarted by her ignorance.

Kay's study of the topography and heroic associations of the two capes,
Palinurus and Misenum, provides literary and historical associations which
magnify their implications for Vergil's readers and for the larger meanings of
the epic. The Copenhagen portrait that graces the cover of Vergilius, is included by McKay in his review of parallel imagines,some of them apparendy
reflected in Renaissance Versions.' Hafner's article is equally relevant to the
continuing scholarly dispute regarding the poet's proper likeness. McKay's
contribution to the Cumaean Bimiilenary Lectures provides details regarding
the establishment of the Vergilian Society at the Villa Vergiliana, an account
of the bimiilenary celebration, and remarks on the topography and monuments of the immediate zone which have implications for the Vergilian text.
Melis and Quilici Gigli treat the Ardeatine territory associated with Turnus,
regions comparable to those saluted by Vergil in A. 6.776: haec tum nomina
erunt, nunc sunt sine nomine terrae. Torelli 's large-scale study of Lavinium
provides some testimony for the Vergilian scholar.

Fasciano's study of numen treats it as an attribute of divinity, and as
synonymous with deus. Quiter's monograph on the implications of the rituals
attaching to the oracular cult marks important advances in understanding.
Schoder's study of Vergil's vision of life is characteristically perceptive and
persuasive. Wiseman emphasizes the importance of Cybele in the Aeneid; her
temple was close to Augustus' own complex, and her treatment by Vergil is in
keeping with the rehabilitation of the Magna Mater. Despite her orgiastic cult,
she is represented as protector of Aeneas and as guarantor of his victory.

Gordon Williams, 'Roman Poets as Literary Historians: some aspects
of imitatiоyìICS 8 (1983) 211-37.
Battegazzore's study of the imagery of fire recapitulates much of Bernard
Knox's incendiary original, and provides fresh material and ideas outside
Aeneid 2. Chênerie's piece on the hexameter is highly instructive. Conte's
diatribe cautions against too much emphasis on echoes and reverberations of
language and on comparative study in Vergilian criticism. Evidence of his own
approach appears in an analysis of A. 10.873-908 (the death of Mezentius)
and of A. 10.540-42, the killing of Haemon. Dangel's articles provide helpful
and directional accounts of her painstaking study of the verse lines. Görler's
bimillenary lecture at Cumae
offers a careful analysis of Vergil's syntax,
emphasizing the way in which the poet displaces adjectives to modify nouns
in unusual contexts. Herein resides the essential art and unique force of
Vergil's lines. La Penna's article on tragic dawns has perennial value. Oksala's
study of Graecisms in the Bucolics merits close reading and development. Ott
continues
to provide useful metrical analyses of Aeneid 5 and 7. Williams

Perspektive

in

BUTTELER FORSCHUNGEN 153-174.
Gigante's introduction to the Lecturae Vergilianae III deals with the fortunes of Vergilian studies under Fascism, with Vergil as vates (transformed)
of Fascist imperialism. Gransden is concerned with the epic as proper blueprint for Milton's Paradise Lost. Hardie argues that the private, melancholy
and pessimistic 'voice' of Vergil emerges in The Divine Comedy not the public
'voice' triumphant and imperial. Vergil's exclusion from salvation accounts
for the 'private' manifestation.
Horsfall and Huxley both
treat the Latin
legend on the new One Pound coin; both recall that the motto derived not
directly from A. 5.262 but from John Evelyn's having seen it in a vignette at
the close of Cardinal Richelieu's Greek Testament (2 Corinthians). Iannucci's
bimillenary lecture in its assessment of the tragedy of Vergil and the virtuous
pagans of Limbo awakens a greater sense of Greek tragedy of necessity than
of Christian tragedy of possibility. Their tragedy is not one of free will but of
predestination. But their tragedy is also a paradox, evoking pity and compassion. 'They were irrevocably scarred by the sin of the First Adam and forsaken by the saving grace of the Second.' Josipovici writes about his play
Vergil Dying, broadcast on Radio 3 (BBC) on 29 March 1979, and subsequently published by SPAN, the Windsor Arts Centre Press. The playwright
records how Vergil influenced him and provides insights into the nature of
the creative process. Kallendorf deals with the popular Quattrocento supplement to Vergil's Aeneid. Because the Renaissance saw Vergil as a source of
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:11:30 UTC
80
virtues and ethical propriety, Vegio sought to tie up Vergil's ethical loose
ends in a thirteenth book. Cristoforo Landino, Kallendorf's other concern,
embodied the best of contemporary Vergilian criticism in his Disputationes
Camaldulenses (1480) and in his line-by-line commentary on the Aeneid
(1488). Lerner's study of the pastoral genre, and its hallmarks (countryside,
language, self-consciousness in the style) is a major contribution. Lorch explains the function of Vergil's Georgics in one of the most important philosophical
tracts
of
the Italian Renaissance. Medcalf explores the gardens,
grotto and lake of Stourhead and detects elements of Vergil's durability and
impression in the places and sculptures of Henry Hoare's gardens. Newman
and Nuttall, in different fashion, explore the classical and Vergilian imprint
on Shakespeare's plays. Nuttall is more concerned with differences and similarities in sensibility in the two poets than with direct influences. Patterson
sees the Eclogues as an ideal text to document cultural history: they are a
virtual emblem, or cultural catalyst in changing times in the successive scholarly commentaries, painterly history, and awareness of social and political
contexts. Patterson's chapter offers bright new directions for Vergilian studies
in this increasingly popular area of cultural and artistic reflections and influences. Pitcher's essay points up frequent borrowings and intimations of Vergil's epic in Shakespeare's The
Tempest. Ross's Sussex lecture deals with
Dryden's Astrea Redux (1660) and his translation (1697), with the illustrators, and with discussions and quotations of Vergil in Steele's Tatler and
Addison's Spectator.
The Seasons, of James Thomson, is addressed at the
close. Sir Walter Scott, in Stovel's paper, favoured Vergilian allusions as an
artistic device, one which would add subtlety, range, and coherence to his
novels. Vance's study of the Victorians balances negative with positive perceptions of Vergil as poet of public and political themes, and as poet of
pathos. Draheim provides a remarkably complete perspective of Vergilian
influence on music from the earliest detections to modern times. Vergil's
influence on the visual arts is a growing concern of scholarship ; Boschloo, de
Jong, and Llewellyn are concerned with Renaissance and later manifestations.
Many will concur with Llewellyn's verdict on Claude's canvasses, that there
was 'either a reciprocal current of influence between text and image or . . .
Claude had a higher degree of Virgilian sensibility than any other artist before
or since.'
